title: 🔥 Example from Understanding Comics
content:
🔥 Example from Understanding Comics
McCloud shows an axe-wielding man in one panel and a cityscape with a scream in the next.
• We never see the act of violence—the reader fills in the gap between panels.

📌 Key Takeaway for Graphic Novels:
• McKee’s conflict model still applies, but in comics, closure (what’s left unsaid) is just as important.
• Instead of showing every moment of struggle, consider implying key moments through juxtaposed images.

🔥 Example in Comics: The Killing Joke
• The final ambiguous panel (Batman & Joker laughing, then silence) forces the reader to decide if Batman killed Joker or not.

3. Escalation vs. Reader-Controlled Time

McKee: Escalation is Non-Negotiable

“A scene must turn. It must begin one way and end in another—otherwise, why is it there?”

🔥 Example from Whiplash
context: From document: 1.1.3. | Narrative Transcends Medium | Subheading — chunk 4 (chunk 4/7)
